did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study evaluated a free-to-use web application that uses artificial intelligence (AI) algorithms to automatically diagnose skin diseases. The AI system processed smartphone images of skin lesions to provide probabilistic diagnoses for conditions such as psoriasis, which were then compared against expert dermatological diagnoses.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a validation study comparing a smartphone application's diagnostic accuracy against expert dermatological diagnoses. It includes specific sections for 'Material and methods' (describing the collection of 150 patients and 900 images), 'Results' (reporting sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy metrics), and 'Conclusions' based on the collected data, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Evaluation of a smartphone application for diagnosis of skin diseases
brief_summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic accuracy of a free-to-use smartphone web application for skin diseases against expert dermatological diagnoses. The research involved 150 patients (900 images), with 100 patients diagnosed with psoriasis, using verified diagnoses confirmed by certified dermatologists and histopathology. The AI application provided five probabilistic diagnoses for each patient, which were compared against expert results to assess sensitivity, specificity, and reliability. Key results showed that the application achieved a sensitivity of 20.00% and specificity of 62.00% for psoriasis, with correct diagnoses appearing in the top five outcomes for only 20.67% of all patients. The reliability, measured by intraclass correlation coefficients, was found to be insufficient for clinical purposes. The authors concluded that while AI diagnostics show potential, the current application is not an adequate replacement for medical professionals due to low accuracy and reliability.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary condition studied; 100 patients (600 images) out of 150 total patients were diagnosed with psoriasis. The application's sensitivity was 20.00% and specificity 62.00% for this condition.

The remaining 50 patients (300 images) had conditions other than psoriasis. The application frequently misclassified these as 'unspecified dermatitis' (n=420), 'folliculitis' (n=154), or 'acne vulgaris' (n=79). The study notes that 27 possible conditions were identified by the app in the patient group, but specific labels for the non-psoriasis cohort are not individually enumerated beyond these common misclassifications.

all_task_types.details: The AI application predicts discrete diagnostic categories for skin diseases. Specifically, it classifies images into one of 27 possible conditions (including psoriasis, eczema, acne vulgaris, unspecified dermatitis, etc.) and provides a probability percentage for each of the top five predicted classes. The study evaluates performance using sensitivity and specificity for binary classification (psoriasis vs. non-psoriasis) and checks if the expert diagnosis matches the most probable, top three, or top five automated diagnoses.

datasets_used.name: Medical University of Lodz Dermatology Cohort
datasets_used.description: A prospective cohort of patients treated at the Department of Dermatology and Venereology, Medical University of Lodz, Poland, between 2018 and 2019. The dataset consists of smartphone images of skin lesions collected from volunteers with verified diagnoses confirmed by two certified dermatologists and histopathological examination. The final study group was formed by removing patients with conditions non-classifiable by the application and adding more data for classifiable conditions.
classes_used: Non-psoriasis skin diseases (including unspecified dermatitis, folliculitis, acne vulgaris, etc.)

use.testing: The dataset was used as the test set to evaluate the diagnostic accuracy of the smartphone application. Each patient's images were submitted to the application, and the application's probabilistic diagnoses were compared against the expert-verified ground truth.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Percentage of correct diagnoses (matched by most probable, top three, or top five outcomes)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To measure precision or relative reliability of the application's diagnoses.
Used alongside ICCs to estimate the precision of the reliability measurements.
To evaluate the diagnostic accuracy for psoriasis (TP/(TP+FN) and TN/(TN+FP)).

statistical_methods_used.method: Positive predictive value (PPV) and Negative predictive value (NPV)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the probability that a positive or negative test result is correct.
To provide an estimate of the likelihood of a correct diagnosis in a non-scientific approach by attributing greater weight to correct diagnoses repeating across samples.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no correlation (or poor correlation) between the replicate measurements (application diagnoses) for the same patient.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant correlation (good or excellent) between the replicate measurements.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The study used ICCs with 95% confidence intervals to assess reliability. A p-value threshold of 0.05 was set for statistical significance. The results indicated that reliability was insufficient for clinical purposes, with ICCs falling into the 'poor' or 'poor to moderate' correlation ranges based on the study's criteria.

Medical University of Lodz Dermatology C.dataset_description: Prospective cohort of 150 patients (900 images) with verified diagnoses. 100 patients (600 images) had psoriasis; 50 patients (300 images) had non-psoriasis skin diseases.

variant_1_top_diagnosis.description: Percentage of patients where the verified diagnosis matched the most probable automated diagnosis.

variant_2_top_three.description: Percentage of patients where the verified diagnosis matched one of the top three most probable diagnoses.

variant_3_top_five.description: Percentage of patients where the verified diagnosis matched one of the five most probable diagnoses.

cumulative_reliability.description: Percentage of patients where the same correct diagnosis appeared in multiple attempts.

reliability_precision.note: Precision rises when considering more diagnosis options, but highest precision for psoriasis was when only the first diagnosis was analyzed.

Medical University of Lodz Dermatology C.notes: Reliability, sensitivity, and specificity were insufficient for clinical purposes. The application is not an adequate replacement for medical professionals.

architecture_details.description: The paper evaluates a 'popular, free-to-use web application' for automatic dermatosis diagnosis. The specific name of the application is withheld by the authors. The application uses an AI algorithm to classify skin conditions. It accepts two images per sample (a full view and a close-up) taken with a 5 Mpixel smartphone camera. The algorithm claims to classify 33 skin conditions, but only 27 were identified in the study cohort.
architecture_details.input_modality: Smartphone images (full view and close-up of skin lesions)
architecture_details.output_format: Five probabilistic diagnoses with certainty percentages

training_details.details: The paper does not report details on the training process, optimizer, loss function, or hyperparameters of the application's underlying AI model, as the study was an evaluation of an existing commercial application rather than a development paper.

classification_or_output_head.details: The application outputs five diagnostic categories with associated probability percentages.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: free-to-use web application (AI algorithm for automatic dermatosis diagnosis, name withheld)
preprocessing_techniques: Removal of irrelevant artifacts such as jewellery or tattoos before uploading images
Checking images for quality before submission
conclusions: The diagnostic accuracy (sensitivity of 20.00% and specificity of 62.00% for psoriasis) and reliability of the evaluated smartphone application were insufficient for clinical purposes.

The probability of the correct diagnosis appearing in the top five outcomes was low (20.67% for all patients and 23.00% for psoriatic patients in two out of three attempts), indicating limited utility as a standalone diagnostic tool.

While the chance of a correct diagnosis increased when more outcomes were considered and more samples of a skin condition were included, the reliability (measured by intraclass correlation coefficients) was poor to moderate, with the probability of a diagnosis repeating for the same patient below 25%.

The application frequently misclassified non-psoriasis conditions, with 'unspecified dermatitis' being the most common incorrect diagnosis (420 instances), followed by 'folliculitis' (154 instances) and 'acne vulgaris' (79 instances).

Although AI diagnostics show potential, the current application is not an adequate replacement for medical professionals due to low accuracy and reliability, and there is a large margin for improvement.

The study highlights that while AI algorithms may be comparable to human specialists for pigmented lesions like melanoma, their value for diagnosing unpigmented skin lesions via popular smartphone applications remains unclear and currently inadequate.
important_remarks: The specific name of the web application was withheld by the authors, preventing independent verification or reproducibility of the exact model evaluated.
The study population was heavily biased towards psoriasis (66% of the cohort), which may have influenced the application's performance metrics and misclassification patterns.

The application's sensitivity for psoriasis was very low (20.00%), making it unsuitable for screening purposes, although the specificity (62.00%) suggests potential utility for ruling out the condition.
Reliability was assessed as insufficient for clinical use, with intraclass correlation coefficients indicating poor to moderate precision and a low probability of diagnosis repetition (below 25%).

The application frequently misclassified non-psoriasis conditions, with 'unspecified dermatitis' being the most common incorrect diagnosis (420 instances), indicating a lack of specificity for differential diagnosis.
The study evaluated a 'free-to-use' application, raising concerns about the generalizability of results to commercial or subscription-based AI diagnostic tools.
The positive predictive value for psoriasis was only 51.28%, which is described as 'nowhere near clinical acceptability' for a diagnostic tool.

The dataset was curated by removing patients with conditions non-classifiable by the application and adding more data for classifiable conditions, potentially introducing selection bias and overestimating performance on the target population.

The application claimed to classify 33 skin conditions, but only 27 were identified in the study cohort, and no list of conditions was available to the researchers, limiting the scope of the evaluation.

High certainty diagnoses (>50%) were rare for correct diagnoses (3.0% for psoriasis) but more common for incorrect diagnoses (11.0% for psoriasis), suggesting the application may confidently provide wrong answers.
